1: The summary of David's campaigns in ch18 closely parallels 2 Sam 8:1-18. The Chronicler preserves these accounts to demonstrate that the conditions necessary for the temple — political security and the accumulation of materials (v8, 11) — were fulfilled during David's reign. wayyaḵ dāwid ʾet-Pᵉlišṭîm wayyiknaʿēm — David defeated the Philistines and subdued them. kānaʿ (hiphil: to humble, subdue) — the same verb applied to Israel's enemies that YHWH promised in 17:10.
2: wayyaḵ ʾet-Mōʾāḇ wayyihyû Môʾāḇ ʿăḇādîm lᵉḏāwid nōśᵉʾê minḥāh — the Moabites became his subjects (ʿăḇādîm, servants) bringing tribute (minḥāh). The term minḥāh (tribute/gift-offering) is also the standard term for the grain offering in the cultic system; its use here for tribute payment from a subjugated nation is a reminder that political submission and cultic offerings share the same vocabulary of acknowledgment.
3: wayyaḵ dāwid ʾet-Hadadezer melek Ṣôḇāh hamātāh bᵉlektô lihyaṣṣîb yādô binhār-Pᵉrāt — David defeated Hadadezer king of Zobah near Hamath when Hadadezer went to extend his hand to the Euphrates River. The Aramean kingdom of Zobah was located north of Damascus in the Beqa valley. The phrase lihyaṣṣîb yādô (to set up his hand/stele) refers to erecting a victory monument — Hadadezer was on a northern campaign that David intercepted.
4: wayyiqqaḥ dāwid mimmennû ʾelep merkāḇôt — David captured a thousand of his chariots. The most significant detail: wayᵉʿaqqēr dāwid ʾet-kol-harekeb wayotēr mimmennû mēʾāh rekeb — David hamstrung (ʿiqqēr) all the chariot horses, keeping only a hundred. The hamstringing of horses was commanded by Moses (Deut 17:16-17: the king must not multiply horses) — David's compliance with this law even in military victory shows his covenantal faithfulness and trust in YHWH rather than military technology.
5: The Syrians of Damascus come to aid Hadadezer but David defeats 22,000 of them. ʾĂrām Dammeśeq (Damascus Aram) — the Aramean city-state of Damascus, which will become Israel's major antagonist in the 9th-8th centuries. Their intervention and defeat marks the beginning of David's empire extending into what is now Syria.
6: wayyāśem dāwid biʾĂrām Dammeśeq — David stationed garrisons (neṣîḇîm, military posts) throughout Damascus Aram. The garrisons convert the territory from a defeated enemy into a controlled province bringing tribute. wayyôšaʿ YHWH ʾet-dāwid bᵉḵōl ʾăšer hālaḵ — the LORD gave David victory wherever he went: the theological refrain that punctuates the military summary (cf. vv13, 14; 17:8).
7: wayyiqqaḥ dāwid ʾēt šiltê hazāhāḇ ʾăšer hāyû ʾal ʿaḇdê Hadadezer — David took the golden shields (šiltê hazāhāḇ, gold quivers or shields) carried by Hadadezer's officers. These luxury items of warfare — gold shields would be ceremonial rather than functional — are brought to Jerusalem, consecrating the spoils of conquest to the city that will house the temple. Their later use as temple ornamentation (implied by v11) materializes the connection between military victory and sacred building.
8: ûmiTibḥat ûmikKûn ʾārê Hadadezer hēḇîʾ dāwid nᵉḥōšet harbēh ʾăšer ʿāśāh Šᵉlōmōh ʾet-hayyām hannᵉḥōšet wᵉʾet-hāʿammûdîm wᵉʾēt kᵉlê hannᵉḥōšet — from Tibhath and Chun, David brought large quantities of bronze — from which Solomon later made the bronze sea, the pillars, and the bronze vessels. The Chronicler explicitly connects David's military spoil with Solomon's temple furnishings — a connection not explicit in 2 Sam 8:8. This is a characteristic Chronicler technique: showing that David's entire reign was preparation for the temple.
9: Tou king of Hamath (Tōʿû melek Ḥamāt) is the Syrian king of the city-state north of Zobah. šāmaʿ — when he heard that David had struck down the entire army of Hadadezer — the verb šāmaʿ (to hear, obey) triggering Tou's diplomatic response: he heard; therefore he acted. International relations in the ancient Near East operated on the news of military success spreading to neighboring states.
10: Tou sends his son Hadoram (Hādôrām, corresponding to Jôrām in 2 Sam 8:10 — a variant name) to greet David and congratulate him. The sending of a son rather than an ambassador signals the highest diplomatic courtesy. The gifts — kᵉlê-zāhāḇ wᵉḵesep ûnᵉḥōšet (articles of gold, silver, and bronze) — follow the standard tribute hierarchy (gold > silver > bronze) used throughout Ch17-18 for cataloging dedicated wealth.
11: ʾōtām gam-hēmmāh hiqqdîš hammelek dāwid laYHWH — King David dedicated all of this to the LORD. qiddaš (piel of qādaš, to consecrate, make holy) — the wealth from all David's campaigns is not merely stored but consecrated (hiqqdîš) to divine use. The accumulation of consecrated materials establishes the treasury from which Solomon will build the temple (1 Chr 29:2-5).
12: wayyaḵ ʾĂḇîšay ben-Ṣᵉrûyāh ʾet-ʾĔdôm bᵉgêʾ-hammelaḥ šᵉmônat ʿāśār ʾālep — Abishai son of Zeruiah killed 18,000 Edomites in the Valley of Salt. The gêʾ-hammelaḥ (Valley of Salt) was a battle site south of the Dead Sea, later the site of Amaziah's victory (2 Chr 25:11). The parallel in 2 Sam 8:13 attributes this victory to David himself; the Chronicler reassigns it to Abishai — possibly reflecting awareness of different source traditions or the complexity of command in a multi-front campaign.
13: Garrisons throughout Edom and the Edomites becoming David's subjects repeats the formula of v6 for Damascus. wayyôšaʿ YHWH ʾet-dāwid bᵉḵōl ʾăšer hālaḵ — the LORD gave David victory wherever he went: the second occurrence of the theological refrain (also at v6 and implied at v14). The phrase serves as a theological frame for what would otherwise read as a purely political-military account.
14: wayyimlōk dāwid ʿal-kol-Yiśrāʾēl wayyᵉhî ʿōśeh mišpāṭ ûṣᵉdāqāh lᵉḵol-ʿammô — David reigned over all Israel and administered justice and equity to all his people. mišpāṭ ûṣᵉdāqāh — justice (mišpāṭ, legal decision, right judgment) and equity/righteousness (ṣᵉdāqāh, that which is right and straight) — the paired terms that define righteous governance in the prophetic vision of the ideal king (Isa 9:7; 11:4; Jer 22:3, 15; 33:15; Ezek 45:9). The Chronicler's summary of David's domestic governance matches the prophets' description of the coming Davidic king.
15: The administrative cabinet of David: Yôʾāḇ ben-Ṣᵉrûyāh ʿal-haṣṣāḇāʾ — Joab son of Zeruiah over the army. wîhôšāpāṭ ben-ʾĂḥîlûd hammazkîr — Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud, the royal herald (mazkîr, one who causes to remember — the royal recorder/secretary of state). The list of officials mirrors comparable lists in other ancient Near Eastern royal archives and signals David's establishment of a professional bureaucracy.
16: wᵉṢādôq ben-ʾĂḥîṭûḇ wăʾĂḇîmelek ben-ʾEḇyātār kōhănîm — Zadok son of Ahitub and Abimelech son of Abiathar were priests. The parallel in 2 Sam 8:17 has “Zadok son of Ahitub and Ahimelech son of Abiathar” — the Chronicler reverses the first names of the two priests' sons, possibly reflecting a textual variant or a known confusion in the sources. Zadok will become Solomon's chief priest (1 Kgs 2:35). wᵉŠawšāʾ sôpēr — Shavsha was the scribe (sôpēr, the royal secretary/scribe), a key administrative position in ancient Near Eastern courts responsible for correspondence and record-keeping.
17: ûḇᵉnāyāhû ben-Yᵉhôyādāʿ ʿal-haKᵉrētî wᵉhaPᵉlētî — Benaiah son of Jehoiada over the Cherethites and Pelethites. The Kᵉrētî (Cherethites) and Pᵉlētî (Pelethites) were David's elite bodyguard units, probably Philistine or Aegean mercenaries — the names may connect to Crete (Kᵉrētîm) and Philistia (Pᵉlešet). Using foreign mercenaries as a royal bodyguard was standard practice in ancient Near Eastern courts, providing a guard loyal to the king personally rather than to tribal or clan interests. ûḇᵉnê dāwid hārīʾšōnîm lᵉyad-hammelek — David's sons were chief officials at the king's side: the royal princes served as senior courtiers, a practice also attested in Egyptian and Assyrian royal households.
